sources.notes: Count colorings of an n by m grid by their coloring cost for exactly k colors.
Official reference code precomputes binomial counts A[x][cost] for color values up to x and applies inclusion-exclusion over the k colors.
Chinese writeup cross-checks the combinatorial layer-counting interpretation for the official formula.

annotation.summary: For every possible cost, count the number of k-color grid colorings with that cost.
annotation.constraints: n,m,k are up to a few thousand in the official implementation, so closed combinatorial sums with precomputed binomials are required.
annotation.core_idea: For a bound x on usable colors, compute A[x][cost]: the number of colorings whose rows and columns expose a particular outer layer size, using binomial choices of nonempty row and column sets and powers of x for lower colors. To count colorings that use all k colors, apply inclusion-exclusion on the missing colors: sum (-1)^j C(k-1,j) A[k-1-j][cost]. Prefix sums over row/column layer counts make each A[x] entry fast to compute after factorial precomputation.
annotation.complexity: O(k(n+m) + k*max(n,m)) after factorial precomputation in the official direct formula.

solution_variants.name: inclusion-exclusion-combinatorics
solution_variants.summary: Precompute color-bound layer counts and use inclusion-exclusion over colors to force using the full palette.

tags.evidence: The solution is an explicit binomial-counting formula for grid colorings grouped by cost.
The final answer enforces all k colors by alternating over missing colors with C(k-1,j).
Prefix sums over binomial row and column counts are used to compute layer contributions quickly.
The official metadata lists FFT, but the reviewed reference implementation uses a direct combinatorial formula rather than FFT as the main solution.
